title: 'Do not buy more than you need': PM urges Aussies not to hoard petrol
text:
P﻿rime Minister Anthony Albanese has called on Australians to stop hoarding petrol, insisting the country's fuel supply is at a healthy level.

Speaking after a meeting of the National Cabinet to coordinate Australia's response to the rising price of oil and petrol caused by the Middle East conflict .

The Prime Minister was adamant the country does not have an issue with the amount of fuel available, and said Australians who were panic-buying petrol were causing more problems than solutions.

Anthony Albanese has called on peoplenot to hoard petrol in order to "help" other Australians. (Nine)

"﻿My message to Australians is do not take more fuel than you need, that is how you can help," he said.

"That is the Australian way, to think of others, to think of their neighbours, to think of their community, but to think of the national interest."

This is despite some regional communities running out of petrol, and with figures of the government hinting that fuel rationing may be required should the global oil shock continue.

However, Albanese batted away these concerns, insisting while distribution was a temporary issue, supply was not.

"﻿There is no less fuel in Australia today than there was three weeks ago," he said.

LIVE UPDATES: Iran pledges to hit back over gas field attack

Long queues for petrol at a Sydney service station. (Peter Rae)

"Every single ship that was due to land here has landed here."

He also said Australia's consumer watchdog, the ACCC, had the full support of the government to crack down on any petrol suppliers attempting to rip off customers at the pump.

﻿"We are cracking down on companies engaging in price gouging," he said.

To help fuel get to all communities in Australia, Albanese announced the creation of a fuel supplier task force, which would work closely with the federal government, as well as all of the states and territories.

The inaugural ﻿coordinator will be Anthea Harris, who was previously the CEO of the Australian Energy Regulator.

Anthea Harris is the coordinator of Australia's fuel supply task force. (Nic Walker)

Albanese said the task force would "support coordination across governments and sectors".

"There will be an agreement between every state and territory [to] appoint someone to work with Ms Harris to make sure that we maximise the coordinated effort during this time," he added.

Albanese called on the states and territories to hold up their end of the bargain to ensure fuel distribution meets the needs of all parts of Australia, especially to support embattled rural communities.

﻿"It is the states and territories that are responsible for distribution within their jurisdictions," he said.

Albanese confirmed the task force had already commenced its work, saying the decision to establish it had been made "two days ago."

title: ACCC to investigation allegations of anti-competitive conduct by regional fuel retailers
text:
The consumer watchdog has announced an investigation into allegations of anti-competitive conduct around the supply of diesel by some of Australia's major fuel suppliers in regional and rural Australia.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) said it has taken the "unusual" step to publicly announce the investigation after it received reports about diesel availability to independent wholesalers and distributors.

The watchdog's investigation into the alleged conduct will focus on Ampol, BP Australia, Mobil Oil Australia and Viva Energy Australia.

The consumer watchdog has announced an investigation into allegations of anti-competitive conduct around the supply of diesel. (Supplied)

READ MORE: Blunt message for Aussies making fuel shortages worse

ACCC chair Gina Cass-Gottlieb said the watchdog would closely scrutinise all fuel markets during this period.

"We have received reports of alleged anti-competitive behaviour," Cass-Gottlieb said.

"We are therefore investigating these matters urgently.

"It is not our usual practice to publicly announce investigations, but given the significance of the issue, the ACCC is confirming this enforcement investigation.

"We recognise the widespread concerns held by consumers, businesses and farmers about fuel pricing and supply issues arising during the Middle Eastern conflict.

"It is important that fuel market participants and the community know that we are closely watching market conduct in relation to all fuels and we will not hesitate to act swiftly to enforce Australia's competition and consumer laws.

"Our investigation is at a preliminary stage, and therefore we are yet to form a view about these matters."

title: Embattled Victorian Premier dismisses leadership speculation from 'scallywags'
text:
Jacinta Allan has quashed speculation she will face a leadership challenge, dismissing the rumours as "anonymous speculation".

With the state election coming later this year, Labor sits behind the Liberal Party in the polls and is also being trailed closely by One Nation.

Media reports have suggested a plan to oust Allan as Labor leader and Premier could be in the making.

Premier Jacinta Allan has claimed "anonymous gossip" from "scallywags" about her leadership is unfounded. (Eddie Jim)

However, Allan denied the rumours, insisting she had the support of the party room and questioning the source of the rumours.

"We are a strong team, we are a united team," she said this morning.

"This is anonymous gossip, it's from a few scallywags."

Despite this, she also sent a pointed message, saying such concerns and rumours would not help Labor's campaign.

"Now is not the time for cuts and division, now is the time to be focused," she added.

Gabrielle Williams insisted the speculation was purely "idle gossip". (Nine)

No one has officially been labelled as wanting to lead the charge of a leadership change at such a crucial moment, with Labor MPs entering parliament today and posing a united front, backing Allan's leadership.

LIVE UPDATES: Iran pledges to hit back over gas field attack

Ben Carroll claimed he was happy to remain in his role as Education Minister. (Nine)

Gabrielle Williams, the Transport Minister from the left faction, and Ben Carroll, the Deputy Premier and Minister for Education from the right faction, both dismissed such speculation when quizzed by media this morning.

"It's idle gossip… I pay no attention to it," Williams said at a press conference this morning.

Carroll was stopped in the halls of Parliament House, where he delivered a similar response.

"No, I want to be the education minister," he replied when asked if he wanted to be Premier.

title: Oil price surges amid fear of escalation in Middle East infrastructure attacks
text:
Australian shares dropped 1.5 percent on opening this morning, a four-month low wiping off about $50 billion as oil prices surged as energy infrastructure on all sides in the Middle East came under attack .

Overnight, oil prices surged again, with brent crude, the global oil benchmark, hitting US$110 a barrel.

The oil surge price sent more shockwaves around financial markets at the same time the White House was doing its best to alleviate fears about rising energy prices.

The global economic fallout remains uncertain as Iran struck Gulf energy sites while Israel took aim at an Iranian gas field. The prospect of an Iran revenge attack for the gasfield bombing will only cause further market carnage.

Gas operations at crucial energy sites in Abu Dhabi have also been temporarily suspended after debris from intercepted missiles fell on the facilities.

What is also uncertain is how much the US backed this latest Israeli strike. The US knew Israel planned to strike Iran's South Pars natural gas fields and oil facilities in Asaluyeh, but it did not take part, the Associated Press reported.

But the Wall Street Journal reported that US President Donald Trump has now made it clear he wants no more strikes on Iranian energy sites, unless Iran threatens oil and trade routes.

Adding to the longtime uncertainty, US Federal Reserve chairman Jerome Powell﻿ said that, ultimately, nobody knows what long-term impact the war will have on the US and the global economy.

"The thing I really want to emphasise is that nobody knows," he said, as the US held interest rates.

Iran keeps up strikes on Gulf countries' oil facilities

Iran has been targeting the energy infrastructure of its Gulf Arab neighbours , as well as military bases, as part of a strategy to drive up oil prices and put pressure on the US and Israel to back down.

QatarEnergy said on X that a missile hit its massive Ras Laffan liquefied natural gas facility, sparking a fire that caused "extensive" damage before it was extinguished.

The company had already halted production there because of Iranian attacks.

Since the war started, a small number of ships have gotten through the Strait of Hormuz, which leads from the Persian Gulf to the open ocean — some Iranian, but also vessels from India, Turkey and elsewhere. Iran insists the waterway is open, just not to the U.S. or its allies.

U.S. President Donald Trump has expressed growing frustration that no allies have offered to help open the strait, posting on social media: "WE DON'T NEED THE HELP OF ANYONE!"

A top British military official, Armed Forces Minister Al Carns, said that any reopening of the strait is a long way off because of threats that include mines, attack boats and drones.

Iraq, which paused operations at its main oil terminal on the Persian Gulf last week, said it had reached a deal with the autonomous northern Iraqi Kurdish administration to begin exporting 250,000 barrels of crude oil daily via pipeline to a port in Turkey.

Saudi Arabia is also bypassing the Strait of Hormuz, sending some of oil by pipeline across the country to be shipped from a Red Sea port.

Qatar says it 'reserves the right to respond' after Iran attacks a key natural gas site

Ras Laffan Industrial City is the largest liquefied natural gas export facility in the world, according to the website of QatarEnergy, the state-owned oil and gas company.

On X, Qatar's Foreign Ministry condemned the attack and said it marked a dangerous escalation. The ministry wrote that Qatar would not hesitate to respond to attacks on its security and sovereignty.

A file image from 2005 of the f Iran South Pars Gas Complex Company. (Corbis via Getty Images)

Why Iran's South Pars field is crucial

The natural gas field under the Gulf is the world's largest. It's shared by Iran and Qatar, and is called South Pars on the Iranian side and the North Field on the Qatari side.

Iran is the fourth-largest consumer of natural gas in the world, and 80 per cent of it comes from South Pars.

Although the field mainly supplies Iran's domestic needs, global oil prices rose on news of the attack due to fears of Iranian retaliation on Gulf energy infrastructure.

Qatar, which has a much smaller population than Iran, has invested billions in developing the field as a source of liquefied natural gas for export.

Iran tried to develop similar LNG exports but was blocked by sanctions over its nuclear program.

title: Study reveals Australia's most popular dog's major flaw
text:
They're among the most popular dogs in Australia, but "designer"﻿ crossbreeds like cockapoos, cavoodles and labradoodles come with a drawback, according to new research.

A study from the Royal Veterinary College in the UK revealed they're worse behaved than their progenitor breeds.

Cocakpoos are bred from poodles and cocker spaniels, cavoodles from poodles and King Charles spaniels, and labradoodles from poodles and Labrador retrievers.

READ MORE: Rideshare giant DiDi increases fuel surcharge for passengers as drivers feel the squeeze

Cavoodles are the most popular dogs in Australia. (Getty)

They're also all very popular in Australia, with ﻿cavoodles ranked as the most popular breed in the country.

Many Australians buy or adopt the "designer" crossbreeds because of the common claim that they have more desirable traits than purebred dogs.

But this new study appears to have debunked that belief.

Researchers surveyed the owners of more than ﻿9400 cavoodles, labradoodles, cockapoos and their purebred progenitor breeds in the UK about their behaviour.

The ﻿survey ﻿included the Canine Behavioural Assessment and Research Questionnaire (C-BARQ), which is used to analyse dog behaviour.

Researchers then compared the different breeds' ﻿C-BARQ scores across a range of different behavioural traits.

The results showed that ﻿in nearly 45 per cent of cases, the "designer" crossbreeds had more undesirable behaviours than at least one of their progenitor breeds based on C-BARQ scores.

Just over nine per cent had fewer problem behaviours.

READ MORE: BHP names a new CEO as Mike Henry heads for the exit after almost seven years

Labradoodles were the only 'designer' crossbreeds that scored better than their progenitor breeds in some areas. (Gsquare, Pixabay, CC0)

Cockapoos are the worst behaved of the bunch, scoring worse than their progenitor breeds in 16 out of 24 behaviours.﻿

Common issues included ﻿owner-directed aggression, stranger-directed aggression, and excitability.

Cavoodles were next with a score of ﻿11 out of 24, followed by labradoodles.

They scored worse than their progenitor breeds in five behaviours, but better in six.

Researchers have called for more research into and wider awareness of potential behavioural issues in 'designer' crossbreeds, especially as they remain so popular.

title: Some Jetstar customers booted from Qantas lounges under new rules
text:
Some Jetstar travellers will be booted from Qantas lounges under new rules coming into effect on July 1.

Customers on Jetstar international flights will no longer have access to complimentary lounge invitations - a single-visit benefit provided by the Qantas Frequent Flyer program or a partner credit card program. ﻿

Customers on Qantas-operated flights, ﻿Qantas codeshare flights operated by Jetstar and Jetstar domestic flights will, however, still have access "where available".

READ MORE: ﻿ Australia condemns Iran as key shipping closure sends fuel prices soaring

The entrance to the Qantas lounge in London. (Qantas)

Complimentary lounge invitations can be linked to flights via an online portal or transferred to another Qantas Frequent Flyer.

Travellers use social media groups to trade their complimentary lounge invitations with each other. ﻿

But the new rules will also block this practice.

Complimentary lounge invitations will soon "only be transferable to someone travelling on the same flight as you"﻿.

Neither of these changes will affect Platinum One Frequent Flyers and Platinum One Lounge Invitations.

9news.com.au understands the changes were driven by customer feedback and ensuring the complimentary invitations are used as intended. ﻿

READ MORE: ﻿ Astronauts had to battle for survival after spacecraft lands in deeply remote forest

The Qantas domestic business lounge in Perth. (Qantas)

Qantas lounges are used by flight enthusiasts to relax, eat and drink before their flight or during a layover.

﻿The airline has lounges both domestically and internationally, including Europe , Asia , North and South America , Africa and the Middle East .

Access is usually granted based on the class of travel or Qantas Frequent Flyer, Qantas Club, oneworld or Emirates membership tier.

Lounges can also be accessed by the complimentary lounge invitation or a single-vist Qantas lounge pass. ﻿

title: Matt took on the King of Denmark in a game of ping pong - and won
text:
T﻿he energy in the room shifts the moment Australia's very own Queen Mary and her husband King Frederik X of Denmark walk in.

You can hear a pin drop despite eager reporters and photographers, including myself,﻿ quietly jostling for the perfect shot inside the tiny room in South Melbourne.

The Danish royals are touring Australia this week – their first visit to Mary's ancestral home since becoming King and Queen in 2024 – and today embarked on a tour of headspace, a national youth mental health foundation.

PETROL PRICES: Rideshare giant increases fuel surcharge as petrol prices bite

Denmark's King Frederik and Queen Mary toured headspace in South Melbourne. (Jason South)

Despite their packed schedule, the couple appear relaxed and happy in Mary's native Australia.

It's no surprise – the couple famously met at the Slip Inn in Sydney during the 2000 Olympics.﻿

Mary and Fred demonstrated an easy rapport with each person they met and with every hand they shook, the tangible excitement in the room grew.﻿

While Mary was whisked away during the visit for a private meeting, Frederik bravely challenged some of the youth advisors in a heated game of ping pong.﻿

﻿Fred played doubles and, despite channelling his inner Marty Supreme with a promising early lead, lost at the final hurdle.

Headspace's Matt was part of the lucky winning team who played a round against the King of Denmark.

He told Nine.com.au he was "very nervous" squaring up against the royal but said Fred was a "gracious" loser.﻿

King Frederik X of Denmark plays table tennis during his visit. (Getty)

Matt was part of the lucky winning team who played a round against the King of Denmark. (Nine)

"I've already told about 10 people that I won and it's been five minutes, I'm very happy with ourselves," Matt said.

"They're both quite lovely," he added.

"You wouldn't pick that they were King and Queen.﻿"

Fred sheepishly admitted to his wife that he lost the game 5-4 when Queen Mary returned to the room later on.

"So disappointing," she joked, prompting a ripple of laughter throughout the press pack.﻿

Queen Mary enjoyed a laugh with the group during her visit. ( Jason South)

Earlier, the royal couple listened intently as the headspace crew spoke about the state of mental health in Australia and the barriers for seeking help.

Mary expressed surprise upon learning it is still hard for some young people to admit they are not OK.

"Is it still very stigmatised?" she asked headspace chief executive Jason Trethowan.

The Danish Queen ﻿is a long-time advocate for mental health and wellbeing, having championing the cause through her anti-bullying organisation The Mary Foundation.

Today marks the monarch and his wife's final day in Melbourne, after visiting Uluru in the Northern Territory and Canberra.

The couple will head to Mary's hometown of Hobart in Tasmania tomorrow, where a warm welcome is expected.

One royal fan had a split-second opportunity to remind Mary of her Tassie roots as she left headspace and greeted the waiting crowd.

"You'll get a great response in Hobart," he told the Queen.﻿

The royals will also host a reception in Melbourne's Royal Botanic Gardens later today.

title: Top tip to save on fuel in Australia's fuel crisis
text:
Aussies all over the country are being hit by rising petrol prices at the pump as the cost of oil skyrockets due to the war in the Middle East .

The average price of petrol across Australia reached an average of $2.19 a litre at the end of last week, with the price of diesel shooting up to above $2.40 a litre.

With petrol needed to keep many Australians moving, here are five tips we can all follow to save money at the bowser next time you fill up.

LIVE UPDATES: Trump berates allies as he finds himself alone on Iran war

Petrol prices showing BP petrol station in Clovelly 16th March 2026. (Steven Siewert)

1. Change your driving style﻿

Experts agree a great and simple way to save on fuel every time you drive is to keep a close eye on your driving style.

If you are constantly speeding up and then having to slow down due to changing speed limits or traffic conditions, you burn more fuel as you have to break more often, and then get back up to speed.

"Heavy throttle use and excessive acceleration are a guaranteed way of using fuel or range," RACV's Andrea Matthews said.

She also suggested using cruise control if your car has it, as this helps maintain a consistent speed without using the brakes or accelerator too much.

The NRMA also suggests carefully planning your route to try and avoid peak periods if possible or roads where traffic will be higher, causing you to be stuck in a pattern of stop-start driving, as well as sitting idle, which burns more fuel.

2. Make sure your tyres are inflated and properly aligned

﻿Not only is having your tyres properly inflated and maintained a good safety measure, it also saves on the amount of petrol you use.

Tyres that are under-inflated increase rolling resistance, and can increase fuel consumption by two per cent, according to the NRMA.

That doesn't sound like a lot, but it can add up over time.

"Underinflated tyres will wear earlier on the edges," Matthews said, "contributing to engine wear and higher fuel consumption."

To ensure your tyres last longer, she also recommends making sure your car has a wheel alignment.

"Getting a regular wheel alignment check will ensure your tyres last as long as possible," she said, saying a poor wheel alignment can produce more wear on the tyres and on other parts of the car, reducing its fuel efficiency.

The NRMA also recommends getting a wheel alignment once a year, as it reduces drag and rolling resistance.

Checking your tyre wear, for example with a 20c coin, is a good way to help save on petrol. (9Honey)

3. Reduce weight by removing roof racks and other heavy items

﻿Science dictates it takes more fuel to move a heavier car, so reducing the luggage and items in your car can help your car remain more efficient.

While there are times you will need to have lots of things in the boot, make sure you do a regular clean out of your car, for example, every time you wash it, to make sure any heavy items that aren't necessary or used often don't take up space and add weight.

According to figures from the NRMA, a car that is loaded to its maximum weight can increase fuel consumption by 24 per cent.

Experts also recommend removing roof racks from your car if they are not being used.

This is because they increase weight, but more importantly, increase drag, making your car less aerodynamic and causing it to use more fuel.

Try to make sure your car boot is not uneccesarily cluttured, as this adds weight and wastes fuel. (Bloomberg)

4. Use your air conditioner less﻿

It may be a big sacrifice to make, especially in a country like Australia where the weather can be extreme, but experts say reducing your air conditioner use in your car is an important way to save on petrol.

The NRMA recommends, when practical, to roll down your windows instead when driving at lower speeds as a way to cool down your car without having to put the air conditioner on, though it warns doing this at higher speeds may have the opposite effect, as it increases drag and resistance.

If you feel the need to use the air conditioner, Matthews recommends these settings.

"Turn the A/C on to recirculate, as a way of reducing fuel consumption," she said.

"That way, you're cooling the same air from inside repeatedly for less money, instead of drawing in hot air from outside."

5. Drive less﻿

While this is not an option for people in all parts of Australia, being more selective on when or how you drive is a useful strategy to save on petrol, especially amid the current crisis.

If possible, carpool with others when driving to work or other events to share the burden of having to drive and use petrol.

READ MORE: Rideshare fares increase as drivers grapple with soaring fuel prices

Try to use public transport if you can to get to work. (Paul Rovere)

If carpooling is not possible and you are able to, use alternative means to get to work or to where you need to go.

Try walking or using a bicycle, or use public transport if you are near a bus stop or train station.

title: Bundaberg residents want buyback scheme after three major floods in 15 years
text:
Flood-affected residents in Bundaberg are calling on the state government to buy back their homes after the third major flood in 15 years.

More than 200 properties were inundated when the Burnett River peaked at 7.4 metres last week.

Rhiannon Harris's family home was one of them.

Rhiannon Harris's family home surrounded by floodwater. (ABC Wide Bay: Johanna Marie)

"It's come through the house pretty much at waist height … everything was destroyed," she said.

With the floodwater having now receded, Ms Harris's home has been stripped bare.

The family's mud-soaked belongings have been left on the kerb, while floorboards have been ripped up and cabinets removed.

Ms Harris lives with her 68-year-old father and two daughters, aged 17 and 23.

Rhiannon Harris's family home has been stripped bare. (ABC Wide Bay: Johanna Marie)

"I've got three people homeless as a result of this," Ms Harris said.

The family of four has been staying with friends, and Ms Harris believes it could be 12 months before they can move back into their home in Bundaberg East.

The city also flooded in 2010, when the Burnett River reached 7.92m and 300 people were evacuated.

Less than three years later, 4,000 homes were inundated when the river peaked at 9.53m.

In March 2026, the Burnett River peaked at 7.4 metres. (Supplied: Cassandra Kirk)

Insurance issues

Ms Harris pays $240 a month for insurance at the moment, but has been quoted $18,000 a year if she wants to maintain a policy.

She wants to see support for residents like her, who are consistently dealing with the impact of floods because of where they live.

"That would be something I'd consider if they [the government] were willing to buy it back … or putting in a scheme that would allow me to raise my home so that I was out of the flood levels," she said.

The Insurance Council of Australia said it was eager for more flood mitigation measures to help drive down premiums.

"Flood [risk] homes are very easily identified. They're the hardest to insure because we know we will be rebuilding or replacing them every five to 10 years," chief executive Andrew Hall said.

Andrew Hall says mitigation projects would help drive down insurance premiums. (ABC News: Craig Hansen)

He said long-term, permanent solutions were needed, and compared the situation to black spots on roads.

"We know where people die on roads and we fix those roads. Why can't it be the same when it comes to flood [risk] homes?

"We should have a plan, whether that is to build a levee, raise those homes, or in worst case scenarios, buy them back and convert them into parkland or industrial estates."

No action on promised levee

In 2023, the federal and state governments committed $175 million to build the Bundaberg East Levee.

The 1.7-kilometre concrete flood levee was designed to protect 600 homes and businesses in the east, south and central suburbs of Bundaberg.

The state and federal governments committed funding for the Bundaberg East Levee in 2023. (Supplied: Tom Smith)

But the project didn't progress because the Bundaberg Regional Council refused to cover ongoing maintenance costs.

Queensland Opposition Leader Steven Miles said the government needed to consider a buyback scheme.

"It's just not fair to keep putting people back into homes that we know will flood again, that we know they will lose all of their belongings again," he said.

"These places have flooded three times now and how many more times are we going to keep putting people back into homes we know will flood?"

But state Disaster Recovery Minister Ann Leahy said her focus was on floodgates.

Ann Leahy would not commit to a buyback scheme. (ABC Wide Bay: Grace Whiteside)

"Here in Bundaberg, obviously, we want to make sure that we have mitigation in place," she said.

"This is a very staged approach in relation to … getting the gates done on that flood mitigation."

The floodgates, which are adjustable barriers that control water flow, are proposed to sit in Saltwater Creek, which runs through central Bundaberg.

Bundaberg Mayor Helen Blackburn said while the community was hesitant about the levee, she hoped residents would welcome floodgates.

Helen Blackburn says she is hopeful the community will be on board with floodgates. (ABC News: Johanna Marie)

" In this particular event that we've had, if we had floodgates, that would have stopped a lot of that water coming in. "

Back visiting her gutted Bundaberg East home, Ms Harris said she was confident her family would bounce back.

"We're a long way off returning, but the light in the tunnel is there. We can see the goal we've got to work towards," she said.

title: Unemployment rate rises to 4.3pc as full-time jobs decline
text:
Australia's unemployment rate rose to 4.3 per cent in February, up from 4.1 per cent, surprising economists.

Economists had forecast about 20,000 new jobs would be created, leaving the unemployment rate steady at 4.1 per cent.

Even though the number of employed people rose by 48,900 to 14,748,700 — more than double what was forecast — the unemployment rate also climbed due to an increase in the percentage of people aged over 15 actively looking for work.

The so-called participation rate rose by 0.2 percentage points to 66.9 per cent.

Follow the day's business news on our live blog.

Some of those extra people looking were yet to find work, so the number of unemployed people grew by 35,000, which contributed to the 0.2 percentage point increase, according to Australian Bureau of Statistics.

"This month we saw fewer people who were unemployed and waiting to start a job in January move into employment in February, compared to recent Februarys," head of labour statistics Sean Crick said.

"We also saw more people remaining unemployed this month compared to recent Februarys."

RBA governor warns of the recession we might have to have Is the RBA fighting last year's inflation war with its latest interest rate hike? Whatever the case may be, Australians are bracing for even more economic pain.

The release of the figures comes after the Reserve Bank board increased interest rates for the second time this year, to 4.1 per cent, pointing to inflation concerns.

The war in the Middle East has led to a spike in global oil prices, driving up costs in Australia.

Reserve Bank governor Michele Bullock said the "board's strategy had not changed", when asked by the media whether unemployment had to increase to help bring down inflation.

"The board's strategy is still to try and maintain as many of the gains in employment as it can," she said.

" We don't want to see a recession or a large rise in unemployment if we can avoid it. "

Loading...

Part-time employment up

While the total number of people employed grew by 48,900, most of that was in part-time jobs.

Part-time employment increased by 79,000 people, while full-time employment fell by 30,000 people.

Why economists hate low unemployment There's a theory that if you have just the right amount of workers on the unemployment scrap heap, you can keep inflation in check.

"This month we saw more people move into part-time employment, particularly those aged 65 and over," Mr Crick said.

"Additionally, this month we saw that fewer people are leaving jobs to retire compared to a year ago."

The growth in both employment and unemployment has resulted in a 0.2 percentage point increase to the participation rate, which was 66.9 per cent in Feburary.

"We recognise that the labour market outlook is starting to look tenuous," KPMG Australia chief economist Brendan Rynne said.

"With a higher interest rate environment and higher cost of living outlook due to increased petrol prices, it seems people are looking to secure their household budgets by increasing their participation in the labour market," he said.

People looking to enter the workforce accounted for part of the increase in the unemployment rate. (ABC News: Steve Keen)

Oxford Economics Australia economist Harry McAuley said the labour showed "early signs of slowing down in February".

"Based on Tuesday's rates decision and the ongoing conflict in the Middle East, we have bumped up our unemployment forecasts," he said.

"We now expect near-term unemployment to rise slightly faster through 2026 and peak at just shy of 4.6 per cent in early 2027."

Loading...

Rates could rise again in May

Annual inflation was at 3.8 per cent in January, but Treasurer Jim Chalmers said there was a "very real" prospect that it would reach 5 per cent this year due to the ongoing conflict in the Middle East.

"With inflation set to approach 5 per cent, the renewed rise in the unemployment in February won't prevent the RBA from hiking rates further," Capital Economics Asia-Pacific head Marcel Thieliant said.

Why did the RBA raise rates as households face petrol price double whammy? This week's rate rise left ABC readers asking how hiking rates would stop inflation caused by unavoidable costs.

He says the increase in the unemployment rate can be explained by a rise in the number of people employed combined with an increase in the participation rate, resulting in almost 84,000 people looking for work.

"As a result, the unemployment rate jumped back up form 4.1 per cent to 4.3 per cent," Mr Thieliant said.

"That's still barely higher than the 4.2 per cent recorded last year and in line with the RBA's forecast that it will reach 4.3 per cent by June.

"Most importantly, the RBA thinks that the labour market is still too tight to meet its inflation target."

Betashare chief economist David Bassanese says signs of a positive labour supply response, along with the lift in the unemployment rate, are likely to be welcomed by the RBA.

"My base case remains that the RBA is on track to raise rates in May, following another uncomfortably high March quarter CPI report in late April," he said.

title: CIT unaware of misconduct investigation into CEO when hired, chair says
text:
The chair of the Canberra Institute of Technology (CIT) has insisted its board did not know its new chief executive, Margot McNeill, was being investigated over allegations of serious misconduct until after she was hired.

Dr McNeill resigned from TAFE NSW, where she was chief product and quality officer, in May last year, and was announced as the new CIT chief executive in June.

The position in Canberra was vacant after the institute's former chief executive, Leanne Cover, was found to have engaged in serious corrupt conduct by the ACT Integrity Commission.

CIT's previous CEO Leanne Cover was found to have engaged in "serious corrupt conduct" by failing to consult with the board about a series of multi-million-dollar contracts. (ABC News: Andrew Kennedy)

Last week, a judgment in an unfair dismissal case was published by the New South Wales Industrial Relations Commission, which revealed an investigation was launched in October 2024 after "multiple complaints from employees" that Dr McNeill had engaged in "serious misconduct".

The investigation concluded late last year, finding that Dr McNeill had breached TAFE NSW's code of conduct, and that her employment file would be updated to reflect that a "termination" in place of a resignation.

The NSW Industrial Relations Commission made no findings in relation to the investigation itself. The case in the commission was ultimately dismissed "for want of jurisdiction".

Case has 'no bearing' on CIT role, McNeill says

In a statement, Dr McNeill said the matter was still the subject of legal proceedings, which included "a challenge to the process, investigation and outcome of events that occurred with my previous employer".

New CIT CEO appointed The Canberra Institute of Technology appoints a new chief executive, a year after Leanne Cover resigned and was found to have engaged in corrupt conduct in her handling of consultancy contracts.

"This has no bearing on my role at CIT, to which I am deeply committed," Dr McNeill said.

"The judgment makes clear that the outcome was created after I resigned from the position to commence my current role with CIT which I won on merit and that there was no dismissal.

"The evidence before the Commission included information about procedural and factual flaws with the investigation and decision-making process, as well as concerns the context in which the investigation was commenced.

" I've been in the education sector for 40 years and am determined to defend my impeccable reputation. "

'We believe she's doing a great job'

CIT board chair Kate Lundy said Dr McNeill did not disclose that she was being investigated over allegations of misconduct until after she was hired.

Ms Lundy said Dr McNeill had since told them she had received legal advice that she was not allowed to disclose anything.

At the time of Dr McNeill's recruitment, Ms Lundy said the CIT board received a "positive" reference for her from TAFE NSW.

She also said the board was still not aware of the nature of the misconduct allegations.

"Where the board is at right now, is we believe she's doing a great job," Ms Lundy said.

"We believe we had a recruitment process that was legitimate.

"At no point were we advised by TAFE NSW that there was an investigation underway.

"After she was appointed, we received an anonymous — a very nasty, vitriolic — letter about her.

"[Then], we of course do what we have to do as a board, which is: you ask the right questions.

"As it turned out, at the same time, the Integrity Commission had received that review.

"It was really only then that we wrote to TAFE New South Wales as well."

Canberra Institute of Technology board chair Kate Lundy said it believed their recruitment process was "legitimate". (ABC News: Lily Nothling)

Ms Lundy said TAFE NSW told CIT confidentiality rules meant they could not disclose information about the issues, but Dr McNeill was open to discussing the matter.

"By then, we understand from Margot [McNeill], that she'd already started legal action and that she was advised not to say anything, but she chose to anyway.

" So all the way along, it's been Dr McNeill that's brought this matter to the attention of the board. "

A TAFE NSW spokesperson said the organisation was committed to "promoting a culture where staff feel comfortable and confident reporting any known or suspected concerns".

"It would not be appropriate for us to comment on the matter beyond what has been concluded and published by the Industrial Relations Commission of NSW," the spokesperson said.

Minister became aware of allegation day before new CEO started

Yesterday, ACT Skills Minister Michael Pettersson told the Legislative Assembly he only became aware of the misconduct allegations the day before Dr McNeill began in her role as CIT chief executive on June 18.

ACT MLA Michael Pettersson said he raised concerns about the impact of the findings against Dr McNeill on community confidence. (ABC Canberra: Donal Sheil)

"I became aware of these matters on the 17th of June, 2025, by an anonymous complaint [received] by the ACT Integrity Commission that was referred to the CIT board, as the matters occurred outside the ACT's jurisdiction," Mr Petterson said.

"On being advised of these concerns, I agreed with the board that the matter should be referred to further authorities.

"I requested the board seek further information from TAFE NSW, I suggested the board seek government solicitor's office advice about the matters.

"I also sought advice on the due diligence undertaken by the board during the recruitment process and an explanation as to how Dr McNeill's involvement in the investigation came to light only after her employment."

Mr Pettersson said when TAFE NSW advised the CIT board it had returned a finding of misconduct against Dr McNeill, he raised concerns about the impact on community confidence and requested an independent review.

"I'll continue to work constructively to ensure that CIT is held in the highest of community confidences," Mr Pettersson said.

"I'm currently awaiting the results of an independent review."

Shadow Education Minister Elizabeth Lee described the events as "extraordinary". (ABC News: Ian Cutmore)

ACT Opposition education spokeswoman Elizabeth Lee said it was an "extraordinary course of events".

"The board chair ... has done nothing to give us any confidence that the board has done everything they should to ensure this didn't happen," Ms Lee said.

"We're talking a very high-level position here, leading a government agency, at a salary of almost half a million dollars.

"At the end of the day, who does the board answer to? And that is the minister.

"The board have a very serious role in this, but he can't escape responsibility altogether."

title: Economy on high alert against backdrop of geopolitical tensions
text:
The outbreak of war in the Middle East means the Australian economy is now in an environment where the risks are "high and rising", according to the latest Financial Stability Review published today.

Despite that, the Reserve Bank insists Australia's financial system is still "resilient" and that the vast majority of households will be able to weather the two interest rate hikes it has announced — so far.

If a major economic downturn were to happen, the nation's banks are "well positioned to absorb significant loan losses while continuing to support the economy through lending to households and businesses", the RBA said.

RBA governor warns of the recession we might have to have Is the RBA fighting last year's inflation war with its latest interest rate hike? Whatever the case may be, Australians are bracing for even more economic pain.

The biggest risk that the RBA identified is the "potential for elevated geopolitical tensions to spill over into a severe international shock".

"The conflict in the Middle East could trigger a larger shock that destabilises the global economy, particularly if supply disruptions to oil and other commodity markets are prolonged," the central bank warned.

Since the United States and Israel's decision to attack Iran in late February, the price of crude oil has spiked well above $US100 per barrel, leading to a rapid surge in Australian petrol prices.

Earlier this week, Treasurer Jim Chalmers conceded this may lead to inflation rising above 5 per cent, if the Middle East war dragged on and the Strait of Hormuz, through which one-fifth of the world's oil flows, remained closed.

"Tensions among major global powers also have the potential to escalate, hostile cyber and other actions are intensifying, and strains in the international rules-based order are increasing alongside the risk of global geo-economic fragmentation," the RBA noted in its report.

Artificial intelligence and 'significant downgrades'

The RBA also flagged the high valuations of artificial intelligence (AI)-related investments to be another major risk to Australia's financial stability.

That is particularly if there is a "sharp revision" on the "productivity benefits" of AI, leading to a "significant downgrade in profitability forecasts and asset valuations".

The RBA has issued two interest rate rises this year. (Reuters: Tim Wimborne)

The bank warned that: "Negative consequences for assets quality in the financial system and investment plans in the real economy could result."

Indeed, another related risk it has previously flagged, which the RBA still sees as a significant issue, is that share and bond markets worldwide "remain vulnerable to sharp corrections" — especially if "the optimistic outlook" of the AI sector is "substantially revised".

RBA to add to petrol pain As Reserve Bank board members gather for the second day of meetings this morning, they already would have been peppered with dire warnings about the potential impact of the energy shock.

Despite that, it said "risk premia" across global markets remains "fairly low by historical standards".

In other words, the RBA is suggesting investors appear to be quite relaxed, largely ignoring the potential risk of a significant market upheaval.

Given that share markets are still close to record highs, despite their recent falls, it is possible people are underestimating the magnitude of the risks their investments could be exposed to.

Over-reliance on China

One of Australia's other major weaknesses is its over-dependence on its largest trade partner, China.

The RBA said China's economy continued to grapple with weak demand, low inflation, a years-long slump in property prices, surging government debt, and "amplified tensions with some trade partners" — without naming the United States explicitly.

ASX falling amid war with Iran. (ABC News: John Gunn)

"A disruptive crystallisation of these vulnerabilities could sharply increase risk aversion in global financial markets and result in reduced demand for Australian goods and services," the RBA noted.

The RBA believes it is not inconceivable that a "perfect storm" of these weak Chinese economic conditions could occur, which would have a significant impact on Australia's economy.

title: Cocaine vessel crew stuck in Sydney Harbour sent to immigration detention
text:
The crew of a vessel that was intercepted in the Pacific Ocean carrying nearly 5 tonnes of cocaine has been escorted off the ship by Australian authorities.

An Australian Border Force (ABF) spokesperson said that its officers boarded the MV Raider this morning and detained all 11 crew members, who have now been transferred to an immigration detention centre.

The ABC understands that MV Raider has been monitored by the ABF since January 2026.

A reason for the crew's detention was not provided.

Australian Border Force officers approached the vessel moored in Sydney Harbour on Thursday morning. (ABC News: Seni Iasona)

The ship was intercepted in international waters by French Polynesia authorities earlier this year and was escorted into Sydney Harbour after issuing a distress call on Friday.

Moored at Snails Bay in Sydney Harbour, the MV Raider's 11 Ecuadorian and Honduran crew members have remained on board since arriving on Friday.

The International Transport Workers' Federation (ITF) said the welfare of the crew was its main priority.

"We expect that the detention would be for the purpose of repatriation and the crew will be afforded fair treatment and due process while ABF arrange for them to be returned home to their families," said Ian Bray, ITF's Australian coordinator.

Mr Bray said the ITF was focused on identifying where the crew members would be detained and obtaining visitation rights for ITF inspectors.

"The ITF will be monitoring the situation and will assess whether the crew will be further assisted by legal representation when it is known if there are any other matters that are being considered or pursued by ABF whilst the crew is being detained," he said.

Crew arrived after 'catch and release'

After intercepting the MV Raider and its crew, French Polynesian authorities allowed them to continue sailing to Australia after dumping the drugs at sea in what was called a "catch and release" scenario.

Mr Bray said the crew signed employment contracts in December last year, which detailed that the MV Raider was to be delivered to new owners in Australia.

The address listed for the new owners belonged to Brisbane-based manufacturer Rosenbauer.

A representative of the company told the ABC that they were not the new owners, and had never heard of the vessel until the ABF contacted them.

"I'd love to know where they've got all their details from the cheeky buggers," they said.

"We sell big fire trucks, this has nothing to do with us whatsoever, it's embarrassing we've got our name on it, I don't know how that happened."

Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke has not commented on the ship's arrival but the crew members were expected to be held in immigration detention while authorities checked whether the boat was seaworthy before their removal from Australia.

Legal experts have said Australian authorities have no jurisdiction to take any action over the cocaine seizure by French officials in international waters.

title: ACCC investigating claims of anti-competitive conduct by fuel suppliers
text:
The competition watchdog has announced it is investigating allegations of anti-competitive conduct by major fuel suppliers in regional and rural Australia.

The Australian Competition and Consumer Commission (ACCC) has taken the unusual step of announcing it is investigating reports about diesel availability to independent wholesalers and distributors supplying regional and rural Australia.

It will probe the conduct of major players — Ampol, BP, Mobil and Viva Energy.

"The ACCC is closely scrutinising all fuel markets during this period, and we have received reports of alleged anti-competitive behaviour. We are therefore investigating these matters urgently," ACCC Chair Gina Cass-Gottlieb said.

Iran war live updates: For the latest news on the Middle East crisis, read our blog.

"It is not our usual practice to publicly announce investigations, but given the significance of the issue, the ACCC is confirming this enforcement investigation. We recognise the widespread concerns held by consumers, businesses and farmers about fuel pricing and supply issues arising during the Middle Eastern conflict.

Fuel price gouging may not be illegal, experts warn Drivers fed up with massive price spikes at the bowser are being warned not to expect a wave of fuel company prosecutions over allegations of price gouging.

"It is important that fuel market participants and the community know that we are closely watching market conduct in relation to all fuels and we will not hesitate to act swiftly to enforce Australia's competition and consumer laws.

"Our investigation is at a preliminary stage, and therefore we are yet to form a view about these matters."

The ACCC called an emergency meeting with the industry earlier this week asking it to justify its pricing.

A spokesperson for BP told the ABC they were engaging with the ACCC.

The ACCC called an emergency meeting with the industry earlier this week. (ABC News: Chris Taylor)

"We take these matters seriously and are reviewing the claims raised. BP will continue to engage with the process as it progresses," the spokesperson said.

"As this is an active investigation, we are unable to comment further at this time."

Prime Minister Anthony Albanese has this morning announced Anthea Harris will be appointed as the federal government's fuel supply task force coordinator.

Ms Harris is the former head of the Australia Energy Regulator and former chief of the Energy Security Board.

The ABC has contacted Ampol, Mobil and Viva Energy for comment.

A report released by the ACCC in November 2025 found imports of petrol had increased over time as domestic fuel refining reduced. (ABC News: Chris Taylor)

Wholesale petrol sales in Australia are dominated by four major suppliers — Ampol, BP, ExxonMobil and Viva Energy.

According to the ACCC, these four suppliers accounted for the majority of wholesale petrol sales in Australia and around 85 per cent of total national wholesale sales volumes between 2023 and 2024.

A report released by the ACCC in November 2025 found imports of petrol had increased over time as domestic fuel refining reduced.

"In 2023–24, imports made up around 64 per cent of total petrol supply volumes in Australia, with the remainder sourced from domestic refineries (around 36 per cent)," the report stated.

title: Dragons forward rushed to hospital in ambulance after training incident
text:
St George Illawarra forward Loko Pasifiki Tonga has been rushed to hospital in an ambulance following an incident at today's training session.

The Dragons forward was seen on a stretcher being assisted by paramedics from the training ground in Wollongong.

In a statement, the club said Pasifiki Tonga had injured his neck and was taken to hospital as a precaution.

"St George Illawarra Dragons forward Loko Pasifiki Tonga was taken to hospital for precautionary scans following a suspected neck injury sustained at training today," the club statement read.



"He was conscious and communicating with medical staff at all times.



"The club will provide an update on his condition once further information becomes available."

Rated one of the club's up-and-coming forwards, Pasifiki Tonga had been listed to play in Sunday's NSW Cup clash against Parramatta.

History against Broncos's NRL title defence if they fall to Storm

The Broncos have lost their opening two matches of the 2026 NRL season. (AAP: Jason O'Brien)

Defending premiers Brisbane are walking a tightrope as they head to Melbourne, desperate to avoid a dreaded 0-3 to start the season.

In the NRL era, only North Queensland in 2015 has bounced back from such a dire start to the season and won the premiership.

The Cowboys, Manly (2021) and St George Illawarra (2005) are the only three teams to have lost their three-season openers and reached the top four. No side has won a competition from outside the top four since the NRL was born in 1998.

Even making the finals from 0-3 is a tough ask. Apart from the above-mentioned three occasions, only the Broncos (1999 and 2007), Sea Eagles (2009), Melbourne (2000), Cronulla (2015) and Warriors (2011) have recovered to make the play-offs after such a poor start.

One notable asset the Broncos had last year was their ability to come back and win games they trailed, thanks largely to fullback Reece Walsh's brilliance.

Reece Walsh was the architect of many Brisbane comeback wins in 2025. (AAP: Dan Himbrechts)

They won once after trailing by 18 points, twice bounced back from 16-point deficits, and on two occasions from 14-point deficits before overturning a 10-point margin in the decider against the Storm.

Their season was also teetering at one point. After the round-13 loss to Manly, they were 11th but won 11 of their next 13 to claim a seventh premiership.

"Everything's pretty calm at the moment. It's not a sprint, it's a marathon," Broncos five-eighth Ezra Mam said.

"We're just at the start of our season now, trying to build to where we want to get to.

"You're not always going to start the way you want to finish."

Brisbane's left-edge defence had holes in it last week in the 40-32 loss to Parramatta, and left centre Gehamat Shibasaki has been relegated to the reserves bench against the Storm as a result.

Winger Deine Mariner reverts to centre and will no doubt be given a working over by the Storm attack.

Brisbane's hopes of a first win in Melbourne since 2016 were hampered further when captain Adam Reynolds withdrew on Tuesday night with a rib cartilage injury.

Veteran Ben Hunt moves from the bench to his preferred halfback position, where he played so well at the back end of last season when Reynolds was out with a hamstring complaint.

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

While history has some stark lessons, it also provides hope for Brisbane.

Broncos legend Shane Webcke reflected on the 1999 season on Channel 7 this week, where as defending premiers they lost their opening five games, and nine of their first 10.

Former coach Wayne Bennett stuck to the blueprint and didn't panic. The players responded and made the first week of the finals.

"That fortified us and was a big reason why in 2000 we won another premiership," Webcke said.

AAP

title: Dance company takes over bowling alley to defy assumptions about disability
text:
It started with a group outing to a bowling alley.

Michelle Ryan, artistic director of Restless Dance Theatre, took a group of people with disability bowling and immediately the staff at the bowling alley began installing gutter bumpers, the inflatable barriers that make it easier for a ball to hit the pins.

The experience inspired her dance theatre production, Guttered, which is being performed this week as part of the Joondalup Festival, inside a real bowling alley.

"I worked in disability services for a while and there's a turn of phrase that's used, which is 'the dignity of risk'," Ryan said.

"You need to allow people to fail to actually grow. And when you go bowling, a lot of people always put up the gutter guards so that people can't fail, and so I thought it was a really nice place to put a show in a bowling alley."

Artistic director Michelle Ryan and performer Michael Hodyl from Restless Dance Theatre. (ABC News: Emma Wynne)

Adelaide-based Restless Dance Theatre has been running for 35 years and champions company members with disability and neurodiversity.

"You'd be surprised that our dancers, whilst there's no gutter guards, they can get strikes. That surprises people, and I love that," she said.

The bowling alleys they have approached have been unexpectedly supportive of the dance company turning their lanes into a theatre.

Michael Hodyl performs in Guttered. (Supplied: Shane Reid)

"At first the bowling alleys are always a little bit surprised at the idea, but they've all been so fantastic," Ryan said.

"The bowling alley here in Joondalup has been extraordinary and they believe in the work."

Creating 'movement phrases' to tell a story

But how does the Restless team express these ideas in dance?

"It's a good question," company member Marcus Louend said.

"We're in a bowling alley so there's lots of stimulation to work with. Obviously there's the act of bowling itself.

"We also work a lot with a task that is different — ways to throw and catch popcorn.

"There's competitive team handshakes."

Michelle Ryan leads a free dance workshop at Currambine Community Centre. (Supplied: Aaron Claringbold)

A big part of what Restless does, in both its performance and public dance workshops is reinforce the idea the movement is for everyone.

That can include taking a simple movement, and challenging themselves to find six different ways to do it.

"In this example it might be: what are six ways you can throw and catch popcorn?" Louend said.

" By the third or fourth example you kind of have to think a bit outside the box, and then you have six, you put them all together and then you have a movement phrase. "

They said to add to the experience, real popcorn is used in the performance and the audience gets to eat the popcorn as well.

"Not the popcorn that's been on the ground, but there will be popcorn for all."

'Anyone can do what we do'

In addition to its performances, Restless has also hosted a series of free, open workshops for people with disability and neurodiversity to come and dance.

"They are extremely popular," Louend said.

"It gives the general public a chance to participate in something artistic, showing that anyone can really do what we do and let their creative liberties flow and express themselves, move their bodies in any capacity."

Restless Dance Theatre company member Marcus Louend. (ABC News: Emma Wynne)

Although the company has been around for 35 years, this is its first visit to Perth.

"We love connecting with community when we come away from Adelaide, and I think it's really important to showcase diversity in all its forms," Louend said.

"By having these workshops, we hope that people will realise what people can do and not always focus on what they can't."

title: Hawks primed to keep 'ball rolling' against red-hot Swans
text:
After ominously kickstarting their AFL campaign, Hawthorn are ready to kick up another level to nullify Sydney's weapons, according to midfielder Conor Nash.

The Hawks had plenty of expectations heading into 2026 after reaching the preliminary final last year.

After delivering a lacklustre opening-round loss to GWS, the heat was on.

But Nash hailed the Hawks' character after an ominous thumping of Essendon, and he believes it can prove a platform for a strong opening chunk of the year.

Swans dealt major injury blow The Sydney Swans will be understrength after losing another key player to injury ahead of facing Hawthorn at the MCG tomorrow night.

"That's the kind of kick-start you need for your season," Nash told AAP after the win over Essendon.

"We've got the ball rolling. We looked like us again there. So pretty happy.

"We know it's just one week, quick turnaround, and we've got a huge test now against Sydney."

Red-hot Sydney, who comfortably won their first two games against Carlton and Brisbane, are a daunting prospect in Thursday night's clash with the Hawks at the MCG.

"They've just got weapons and threats everywhere — and particularly in midfield, that's where they've got heavy reliance, heavy dominance," Nash said.

"We're gonna have to bring our A--game.

"It's gonna have to be up to top notch. We know it'll go to another level again.

"But we front up every week, and we're looking forward to it."

ABC Sport Daily podcast ABC Sport Daily is your daily sports conversation. We dive into the biggest story of the day and get you up to speed with everything else that's making headlines.

The Hawks' midfield of Jai Newcombe, Nash, Josh Ward and Cam Mackenzie got the job done in the clinches against Essendon.

But Sydney — albeit without stars Errol Gulden (shoulder) and Isaac Heeney (hamstring) — still has the likes of Chad Warner, Justin McInerney and James Rowbottom to contend with.

"They've got threats everywhere. We'll have to have a plan for those boys," Nash said.

"The biggest part will be getting it done together as a group.

"That's the beauty of our midfield. We try to spread the load. It's a real workmanlike midfield."

AAP

title: Parking politics and 'crappy' bike lanes stifling a cycling boom in Sydney
text:
Cars raced towards Sophie Duxson as she lay in the middle of the road in Sydney's inner west.

Ms Duxson, pictured above, was riding across an intersection on the Carrington Road cycleway in Marrickville when a car pulled out and hit her, knocking her to the ground.

"I remember lying there thinking, what a stupid way to die," Ms Duxson says.

This time, at least, she survived. The incoming traffic had enough time to slow down and stop in front of her.

Eight months later, she's still recovering from whiplash to her neck and shoulders and has been too afraid to ride her bike again.

The intersection of Carrington Road and Renwick Street where Ms Duxson was hit by a car. (ABC News: Aston Brown)

The cycleway she was using was initially proposed on the other side of the road to avoid it crossing over five busy T-intersections, including the one where Ms Duxson was hit.

But that plan was shelved, largely due to concerns over the loss of on-street parking.

Today that stretch of kerb is lined with a few parked cars, a trailer, dumpsters and a smashed-up vehicle.

A badly damaged car parked on the kerbside of Carrington Road, where the cycleway was originally proposed. (ABC News: Aston Brown)

"Imagine if you dumped a huge chest of drawers on the side of the road, and you said to everyone, it's my right to have my private property on the street," Ms Duxson says.

" What is it about cars that makes it OK for people to do that? "

Following safety concerns expressed by cyclists since the cycleway opened in 2014, in 2024 the Inner West Council added strips of green paint and new signs at the intersections to make the cycleway safer.

The cycleway was put on the other side of the road to minimise parking loss. (ABC News: Aston Brown)

"It's an improvement but it's just paint, you're still vulnerable, like a sitting duck," Ms Duxson says.

In a statement, an Inner West Council spokesperson said the Carrington Road cycleway was built to the safety standards at the time, as dictated by the state government transport department, Transport for NSW.

Bins, dozens of parked cars and broken down vehicles line the kerb on Carrington Road. (ABC News: Aston Brown)

They said the cycleway was upgraded after the department changed their design requirements.

And they said the council "endeavours to balance competing demands to achieve the best overall result [for cycleways]".

The intersection of Carrington Road and Harriet Street. The Carrington Road cycleway crosses over five wide intersections with fast moving-traffic. (ABC News: Aston Brown)

Patchy and poorly designed

Ms Duxson's crash points to what cycling advocates and experts say is a broader problem in Sydney's inner west.

They say the area is a sparse and often poorly designed network of bike lanes that often disappear at busy intersections, forcing riders into traffic or footpaths.

Urban designer and researcher with University of New South Wales Mike Harris says Sydney's inner west has exactly the conditions where cycling should thrive.

He says the suburbs are dense and growing and many residents, including a high proportion of students and young professionals, live within cycling distance of jobs in the CBD.

Dr Harris says the reality is it is mostly too dangerous for bike riders to ride confidently.

Bike lanes in Sydney’s inner west often end suddenly at intersections, creating a conflict with riders and people walking. (ABC News: Aston Brown)

Meanwhile he says in the neighbouring City of Sydney, cycling is flourishing as a convenient, fast and safe way to get around.

Dr Harris says the disparity is most clearly reflected in infrastructure.

With a strong political mandate and relatively large budget, City of Sydney council has built almost 27 kilometres of on-road separated cycleway since its first modern bike lane opened in 2009.

There is about 5 km of separated bike lanes in the Inner West Council area and riders say parts of the limited network are poorly designed and dangerous. The Carrington Road cycleway was the first constructed.

Map of the Inner West and City of Sydney Council areas showing the disparity between separated cycleways/bicycle paths. (ABC News: Dylan Goolagong/Bicycle NSW)

By land area, the City of Sydney has roughly seven times the density of separated cycleways as the Inner West Council area.

"All the research tells us that until you provide safe, separated infrastructure, most people feel too unsafe to ride," Dr Harris, who studies cycleway developments in cities around the world said.

Parking turns 'neighbour against neighbour'

The president of advocacy group Bike Marrickville, Ellis Bou-Harb, pitches new cycling infrastructure as a cost-effective way to improve the lives of the whole community, not just for people who want to ride bikes.

"It's about improving public health, reducing pollution and traffic, allowing people to do incidental exercise and feel safe," Mr Bou-Harb says.

But not everyone is convinced.

Independent Inner West Council councillor Victor Macri stands on the Livingstone Road cycleway, which he campaigned against. (ABC News: Aston Brown)

Inner-west independent councillor Victor Macri has been cutting hair at a barber shop on Marrickville Road for most of his working life.

"When people come in the one thing they complain most about is parking," Cr Macri says.

" It's turning neighbour against neighbour. "

Cr Macri says he supports cycling in principle but does not support separated bike lanes because they may reduce the amount on-street parking, which he says residents are "entitled" to.

Cr Macri fought to retain both sides of street parking, at the expense of the quality and safety of the cycleway. (ABC News: Aston Brown)

In 2016, Cr Macri rallied residents living on the road to campaign against the then-proposed Livingstone Road cycleway because it planned to remove one side of on-street parking to make enough room for the bike path.

According to the draft concept plans, those parking losses would have been offset by new angled parking on nearby side streets, netting an overall gain of up to 19 on-street car spaces.

Almost all houses on the road have access to private off-street parking.

Even so, the design was altered to retain both sides of street parking due to pressure from Cr Macri's campaign.

The Livingstone Road cycleway was designed this way to retain both sides of on-street parking along the road. (ABC News: Aston Brown)

After years of consultation and planning, the 1.4km cycleway was opened in 2022. Because of the design changes the cycleway is fragmented into six separate stretches of bike lane instead of a single continuous path as originally proposed.

Fiona Campbell, a volunteer at Lewisham Better Streets who also works as City of Sydney's cycling strategy manager, says the retention of both sides of on-street parking "compromised" its quality and safety.

"It requires people riding to veer in and out of the footpath, putting them into conflict with people walking," Ms Campbell says.

An uphill battle

Kobi Shetty, a local state government MP and Greens spokesperson for active transport, says the "really crappy" bike lanes in Sydney's inner west reflect the "car-centric attitudes" that dominated the Inner West Council's transport department during her 2021-2024 tenure as a councillor.

Greens Member for Balmain, Kobi Shetty riding an e-bike with her children Maya and Xavi, and their dog, Zola in Lilyfield. (ABC News: Aston Brown)

In 2023 the council released an ambitious cycling strategy, but Ms Shetty says that has not been backed up by adequate resources.

"That council in particular is very much controlled by the mayor and he doesn't seem to have much interest in cycling," Ms Shetty says.

A separated bike path was first-proposed along Lilyfield Road more than a decade ago, but is still yet to be constructed. (ABC News: Aston Brown)

Darcy Byrne, the Inner West Council mayor, refuted claims that building pedestrian and cycling infrastructure (known as active transport) was a low priority under his leadership.

He says the completion of the much-loved GreenWay — a new 6km walking and cycling shared path through the heart of the inner west, demonstrates the council has an appetite for "visionary" active transport projects.

The GreenWay, a 6km walking and cycling path which opened in December 2025, is widely-considered a huge success. (ABC News: Aston Brown)

"The GreenWay is a beautiful thing," Mr Bou-Harb adds.

" It's a safe space completely protected from cars that enables people to gain more confidence on a bike, or kids that want to learn how to scoot or rollerblade. "

However, Cr Byrne says that the area's hilly geography and narrow streets make building on-road cycle paths a challenge.

"We have some of the oldest roads in Sydney, they weren't designed for active transport … it can be extraordinarily dangerous for cyclists on non-dedicated lanes in the inner west and all over Sydney" Cr Byrne says.

The Cooks River walking and cycling path is linked to the GreenWay, creating kilometres of running or riding far from roads. (ABC News: Aston Brown)

Cr Byrne says it would be "completely unfair" to compare City of Sydney's ambitious bike lane rollout to neighbouring councils like the inner west because they have far smaller budgets.

Funding 'not aligned' with emissions reduction targets

Beyond local disputes over parking and road space, experts and advocates say chronic underinvestment is a major barrier.

According to Bicycle NSW, less than 1 per cent of the state government's transport budget is allocated to active transport.

The United Nations recommends that 20 per cent of transport budgets go towards active transport to combat climate change and improve public health.

In response to the 2025 Parliamentary Inquiry into the use of e-bikes, the NSW government committed to this recommendation in principle.

At the current level of funding and rate of delivery, Dr Harris says it would take centuries to build a comprehensive bicycle network across Sydney.

"When we think about making our cities less car dependent to live healthier lives and reduce our carbon emissions, clearly that time frame is not aligned with those goals," Dr Harris said.

In a statement, NSW Transport Minister John Graham said the government is committed to building more walkways and cycleways, but that those investments must be "balanced" with other "key priorities" in the budget.

"Sydney is increasingly becoming a cycling city. We've got record numbers on new cycleways and we're now the second highest users of Lime bikes in the world behind London," Mr Graham said.

title: Why did the RBA raise rates as households face petrol price double whammy?
text:
The Reserve Bank hiked interest rates for the second time this year this week, but the closely divided vote indicated it wasn't an easy call.

The hike adds an extra cost to many households and businesses that are already facing another kind of (unavoidable) price pressure in the form of higher fuel prices.

And it left ABC readers asking — how would hiking rates stop inflation caused by an unavoidable cost like petrol that's spiking due to a war on the other side of the world?

And if the RBA wants households to tighten their belts, isn't the fuel price hike going to do that for them anyway?

As the split in the RBA boardroom indicates, there are no easy answers — but let's walk through what we know.

Why did the RBA board raise interest rates?

In short — because inflation is too high for its liking.

Top RBA officials have been vocal about the need to bring down inflation, after it picked up in the second half of 2025.

The inflation rate measures increases in the prices of goods and services, and the central bank is tasked with keeping prices low and stable.

To achieve this it tries to keep the inflation rate between 2 to 3 per cent.

The latest data shows consumer price rises are well above the RBA's target, with headline inflation up to 3.8 per cent in January.

Of course, the RBA's monetary policy board was also meeting against the backdrop of war in the Middle East, which has disrupted the flow of oil and gas through the key Strait of Hormuz through which about a fifth of the world's seaborne oil supply passes.

The government says fuel shipments continue to come into Australia as expected, for now, and fuel distributors have reported that supply disruptions are resulting from skyrocketing demand, leading to warnings against panic buying.

But it's cold comfort to motorists facing price pain at the petrol pump.

In her post-meeting press conference, RBA governor Michele Bullock emphasised that there were reasons to raise interest rates before taking into account the crisis in the Middle East.

"Higher petrol prices will add to inflation, but they're not the reason for today's decision," she said.

The spike in petrol prices comes with a spike in mortgage payments for many Australians. (ABC News: Dan Irvine)

A key reason for raising rates, the governor explained, was a mismatch between supply and demand in the economy — based on the recent run of data showing Australia's economy growing faster than expected, a steady jobs market, and above-target inflation.

"Taken together, the data suggests there is slightly more excess demand in the economy than we thought in February, and inflationary pressures are therefore somewhat greater," Ms Bullock said.

Note she says slightly more — so it's not like we're in a situation where demand is running out of control.

Loading...

How do higher rates bring down inflation?

The Reserve Bank raises the cash rate to increase borrowing costs for Australian households and businesses, through higher interest rates on their loans.

By changing how much cash is available to spend, by making it more expensive to borrow money, that influences people's behaviour and, therefore, the demand in the economy.

And when demand drops, prices should go down, in theory.

After Tuesday's rate hike, households with variable home loans will be left paying between tens and hundreds of dollars more on their monthly minimum repayments — that's money they don't have to spend elsewhere.

If businesses have fewer people willing or able to buy their goods or services then they will be less able to keep increasing prices, even as their own costs may be rising.

The RBA governor pointed out on Tuesday that there were a few key so-called transmission channels for interest rates to influence the economy.

The first is by crunching cash flow, as we just described, and it's the one that gets the most attention.

"That is, those people who have mortgages pay more on interest, and that means that they have less spare money," Ms Bullock explained.

Another channel, the governor said, is encouraging people to save more and spend less now, as they can earn more interest on their money in the bank — and in a similar way to the cash flow channel it takes demand out of the economy.

And finally, the exchange rate. All else being equal, if Australia's interest rates are higher compared to overseas counterparts it lifts the value of our currency.

That's because a country where your money can earn more interest is an attractive place to park your cash and sees more dollars flow in.

If our exchange rate rises, imported goods become cheaper.

"People tend to buy imported goods rather than domestic so it puts less pressure on the domestic side of things," Ms Bullock said.

The cost of filling up is hitting many Australians hard. (ABC News: Dan Irvine)

But knowing the way higher interest rates typically flow through the economy may raise more questions than answers about what's going on at the moment.

It certainly did for many ABC readers who commented on our blog or social media posts, or wrote in.

Is the RBA going to lift rates to … encourage world peace and stop the war in Iran? When the data is so clearly not linked to consumer spending, what is the point of raising rates? - Sophie

So given that the Middle East/oil inflationary pressures are completely out of our control, what does this rate hike do other than punish people with (large) variable mortgages? Bumping up interest rates won't make Iran open up shipping. - Ben

For many households and businesses fuel isn't an avoidable expense. So rate rises aren't going to cut demand for fuel.

Demand for other goods and services is likely to fall if people have less cash because they're spending more on their home loan and petrol tank.

But did the RBA need to raise rates then? Or would the rise in petrol have done its job for it anyway, acting as another quasi-rate hike on households by crunching cash flow?

Did rates need to rise if petrol is also hitting households?

That's a question that's difficult to answer without a crystal ball — or a direct line into the mind of Donald Trump, perhaps.

It's one the RBA's rate-setting board grappled with over two days of what Ms Bullock described as "robust" discussion this week.

Michele Bullock announced the rate increase on Tuesday. (AAP Image: Dan Himbrechts)

She was at pains to emphasise the split among board members — with five voting to hike rates in March and four calling to keep them steady — was not a dispute around whether rates should go up, but about the timing.

A rate hike had previously been pencilled in for May by many leading economists, but the conflict and associated oil price spike had many shift those forecasts forward to March while still expecting a third straight rate hike in May.

In recent weeks, both Ms Bullock and her deputy Andrew Hauser made public remarks about the RBA watching the Middle East situation closely and acknowledging the risk to inflation.

"You have the immediate effects on the cost of petrol at the pumps. Everyone's seeing that in real time today," Mr Hauser told The Conversation's Michelle Grattan.
